content.assessment: Eliminating the age-10 and age-12 entry points directly targets the mechanism most strongly and consistently linked to inequality in the evidence base: early tracking via 6- and 8-year gimnázium programs. Removing early selection would collapse the stratification funnel to a single later transition point, which theoretically reduces the window during which family socioeconomic status can determine track placement, addressing the strong finding that SES explains over 20% of achievement variance partly through school-type segregation. However, whether delaying selection reduces the underlying segregation mechanism (which is also driven by pre-existing ethnic/Roma residential and school segregation) or merely relocates sorting to age 14 remains uncertain within this evidence set.
opportunities: A single later selection point could reduce cumulative sorting exposure and narrow the socioeconomic gradient if segregation is substantially concentrated in the early-entry mechanisms
strengths: Directly removes the two earliest selection points most implicated in stratification by family socioeconomic status
Addresses the structural channel (school-type segregation) identified as a major contributor to Hungary's exceptionally strong socioeconomic achievement gradient
threats: Selection pressure and sorting by SES could simply relocate to age 14, preserving segregation outcomes if underlying demand-side drivers (family strategy, distrust of comprehensive schools) remain unaddressed
Existing residential and ethnic segregation patterns, which operate independently of gimnázium tracking, would likely persist unchanged
weaknesses: Achievement and segregation gains from short-term selection advantages at the individual level are not clearly established, so removing early selection may not automatically translate into proportionate long-run mobility improvements for those previously excluded
Delaying tracking does not itself address the deeper Roma/ethnic segregation already embedded independently of gimnázium selection
